content.title: Fig. 3. The proportion of people exposed to  PM_{2.5}  in BTH, PRD, YRD, FWP region and the whole nation at different years (i.e., 2014, 2017, and 2020). The dot vertical lines denote the air quality targets of WHO (i.e., IT-1, IT-2, IT-3, IT-4 and AQG of annual mean concentrations of 35, 25, 15, 10, and  5\mu gm^{-3} ).
content.summary: The figure illustrates the proportion of populations in different regions of China exposed to PM2.5 levels compared to WHO air quality targets for 2014, 2017, and 2020.
content.insight: Significant reduction in populations exposed to high PM2.5 levels, with BTH showing most improvement, yet most still exceeded WHO IT-3 in 2020, indicating ongoing public health challenges.

metadata.title: Fig. 3. The proportion of people exposed to  PM_{2.5}  in BTH, PRD, YRD, FWP region and the whole nation at different years (i.e., 2014, 2017, and 2020). The dot vertical lines denote the air quality targets of WHO (i.e., IT-1, IT-2, IT-3, IT-4 and AQG of annual mean concentrations of 35, 25, 15, 10, and  5\mu gm^{-3} ).
references: Fig. 3 shows the population exposed to ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in pollution control priority regions and in the whole nation in specific years at the beginning and end of the two phases of ACP (2014, 2017, and 2020). In 2014, over  $80\%$  of Chinese people lived in areas with  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  over  $35\mu \mathrm{g}$ $\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  (WHO IT-1). In 2020, the corresponding proportion decreased to  $40\%$ . BTH had the most severe  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, even with the most prominent air quality improvement amongst four priority regions. In 2014, almost all populations in BTH lived in areas where  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeded the WHO IT-1 threshold and this population proportion decreased to  $90\%$  in 2020. The FWP was not considered as a priority region for air pollution control in APPCAP, and pollution status was more serious during APPCAP as a result of crude industrial production and its gully terrain that was not conducive to the diffusion of pollutants. By contrast, the effect of ACP measures was profound in FWP region under APWBSD with a  $10\%$  reduction in the population exposed to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5} > 35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  in 2020 compared to 2017. The exposed population in PRD is relatively low compared to other priority regions, with  $24\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2014 and  $100\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2020, which also achieved the "Beautiful China" vision. However, almost all populations ( $\sim 98\%$  in the whole nation) still lived under the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeding the WHO IT-3 in 2020, not to mention the WHO AQG standard.

metadata.title: Fig. 1. The averaged  PM_{2.5}  concentrations during APPCAP (a) and APWBSD (c), and their change in APPCAP (b), and APWBSD (d).
references: After seven-year implementing of Air Clean Plan, the mean  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations clearly decreased  $(13.41\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ $33.65\%)$  in China. The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in the first phase (2014-2017) were 35.97  $\pm 15.11~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ , which decreased to  $28.92\pm 12.76~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$  in the latter phase (2018-2020) of the China ACP. In both phases, high concentrations were observed mainly in northwest China, northern China, and a small proportion of central China (i.e., Henan and Hubei provinces) (Fig. 1). The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations decreased in over  $68.09\%$  of the area in mainland China during the period of APPCAP (Fig. 1b). A clear reduction was detected in BTH (decreased by  $17.33~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $25.81\%$ ), followed by YRD (decreased by  $12.79~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $24.07\%$ ) and parts of central China (decreased by  $14.66~\mu \mathrm{g~m}^{-3}$ ;  $22.93\%$ ) during the APPCAP

Fig. 3 shows the population exposed to ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in pollution control priority regions and in the whole nation in specific years at the beginning and end of the two phases of ACP (2014, 2017, and 2020). In 2014, over  $80\%$  of Chinese people lived in areas with  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  over  $35\mu \mathrm{g}$ $\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  (WHO IT-1). In 2020, the corresponding proportion decreased to  $40\%$ . BTH had the most severe  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, even with the most prominent air quality improvement amongst four priority regions. In 2014, almost all populations in BTH lived in areas where  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeded the WHO IT-1 threshold and this population proportion decreased to  $90\%$  in 2020. The FWP was not considered as a priority region for air pollution control in APPCAP, and pollution status was more serious during APPCAP as a result of crude industrial production and its gully terrain that was not conducive to the diffusion of pollutants. By contrast, the effect of ACP measures was profound in FWP region under APWBSD with a  $10\%$  reduction in the population exposed to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5} > 35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  in 2020 compared to 2017. The exposed population in PRD is relatively low compared to other priority regions, with  $24\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2014 and  $100\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2020, which also achieved the "Beautiful China" vision. However, almost all populations ( $\sim 98\%$  in the whole nation) still lived under the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeding the WHO IT-3 in 2020, not to mention the WHO AQG standard.

During the period of APPCAP, the control policies essentially contributed to the decrease of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations (Fig. 8a). The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  reduction was sourced primarily from the clean production policy (net decrease contribution of  $10.53\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by the energy consumption control measure (net decrease contribution of  $7.28\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) and fuel structure optimization (net decrease contribution of  $3.08\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ). Economic growth was the primary driver for increasing  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution from 2014 to 2017, with a net contribution of  $9.00\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The co-effect of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in this period also intensified  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution. During the APWBSD, the contribution of clean production, energy consumption control and fuel structure optimization to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  decrease were  $85\%$ ,  $37\%$  and  $21\%$ , respectively. Compared with the period of ACPCAP, the effectiveness of all measures decreases in APWBSD, especially the clean production measures (from  $-257\%$  to  $-85\%$  ) and

We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\sim 27.05\%$  of global ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\%$  and  $26\%$  decrease in the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors.

Our analysis of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  attributed deaths in China was compared with several recent studies (Table S3). Great progresses was made in previous studies (Zhang et al., 2019a; Li et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2019; McDuffie et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2017; Zou et al., 2019; Apte et al., 2015), albeit with several limitations in common: (1) previous studies focused mainly on the APPCAP (2013-2017) and lack the updated results of the APWBSD (2018-2020); (2) the chemical transport model was used widely in previous research, which showed advantages for predicting the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations without ground measurement, but also has limitations in terms of prediction accuracy (Ma et al., 2022); (3) the Integrated Exposure Response (IER) model used for premature death estimation in previous studies lacked sufficient information on deaths at high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and failed to separate exposure from ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and secondhand smoke, which could potentially introduce uncertainties (Burnett et al., 2014). Our research provides several advances compared to previous studies. First, we quantified the co-benefit of the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measures in China for the environment and health from 2014 to 2020. Second, we used state-of-the-art  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products with the highest accuracy (Wang et al., 2021; 2022) (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4 for detail) and the finest resolution (spatial resolution of  $1\mathrm{km}$ , Fig. S2) at the national scale to offer a full-coverage estimation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. With these innovations, we not only provided a more accurate estimation of the risk due to exposure to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , but also to analyze the great spatially heterogeneity of policy effectiveness. Third, we adopted the updated MR-BRT spline method, which fits the risk with a more flexible shape for more reliable estimation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths (Hystad et al., 2020). The MR-BRT includes more exposure scenarios at high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels during fitting compared with other models such as the IER model (Yusuf et al., 2020; Chowdhury et al., 2022). It also excludes active smoking to control for and reduce potential bias due to this effect (Pope et al., 2018). Finally, our study estimated the co-benefit from  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  to the environment and health under the background of the ACP and NDC pledges for the first time.

metadata.title: Fig. 8. Contribution of different factors to the change of  PM_{2.5}  concentrations (a) and  PM_{2.5} -attributable premature deaths (b) in 2014 to 2020 ("Clean production") denotes process control measures, "Optimize fuel structure" denotes structure control measures, "Energy consumption control" denotes source control measures, "Co-effect of C and PM" denotes the co-effect of  CO_{2}  and  PM_{2.5} , "Death rate" denotes health care level).
references: China implemented a stringent Air Clean Plan (ACP) since 2013 to address environmental and health risks caused by ambient fine particulate matter  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . However, the policy effectiveness of ACP and co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures to environment and health are still largely unknown. Using satellite-based  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products produced in our previous study, concentration-response functions, and the logarithmic mean Divisia index (LMDI) method, we analyzed the spatiotemporal dynamics of premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and quantitatively estimated the policy benefits of ACP and carbon mitigation measures. We found the annual  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in China decreased by  $33.65\%$ $(13.41\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  from 2014 to 2020, accompanied by a decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths of 0.23 million  $(95\%$  confidence interval (CI): 0.22-0.27), indicating the huge benefits of China ACP for human health and environment. However, there were still 1.12 million  $(95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by the exposure of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in mainland China in 2020. Among all ACP measures, clean production (contributed  $55.98\%$  and  $51.14\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) and energy consumption control (contributed  $32.58\%$  and  $29.54\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) made the largest contribution during the past seven years. Nevertheless, the environmental and health benefits of ACP are not fully synergistic in different regions, and the effectiveness of ACP measures reduced from 2018 to 2020. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  has become one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction since 2018, confirming the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Our study suggests, with the saturation of clean production and source control, more targeted region-specific strategies and synergistic air pollution-carbon mitigation measures are critical to achieving the WHO's Air Quality Guideline target and the UN's Sustainable Development Goal Target in China.

The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure-related drivers (i.e., the drivers of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) avoided 0.10 million net premature deaths in the first phase (2014 to 2017) (Fig. 8b). The measures in ACP fully offset the increase in the number of premature deaths caused by the co-effect of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  as well as the population growth effect, indicating the explicit positive health effect of air quality improvements. The vulnerability factors (i.e., health care level and age structure) contributed to 0.03 million net premature deaths during 2014 to 2017. Amongst them, the improvement in health care level (decrease of baseline death rate) cannot fully offset the net deaths caused by the aging population in China, meaning that the health care level in China needs to be enhanced further. During the APWBSD, improvement in health care became a more effective driver than energy consumption control for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission to avoid  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable deaths, and can, thus, fully offset the effect of population aging. The population aging caused more premature deaths than economic growth, in the phase of APWBSD, which was distinct from APPCAP.

We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\sim 27.05\%$  of global ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\%$  and  $26\%$  decrease in the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors.

metadata.title: Fig. 5. Trend of annual average  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for the top 20 most populated cities in China from 2013 to 2018. Observed  PM_{2.5}  concentrations were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.
references: Guided by the Action Plan, many other cities in China are following Beijing to reduce ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations (Fig. 5). The strategic promotion of ecological civilization has spurred stronger willingness, clearer targets, more supportive legislation and incentives, and more stringent implementation of control actions from central to local governments. All these factors work together with public engagement to achieve progress in controlling  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution and improving public health and welfare. During the Action Plan 2013-2017,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations deceased by  $23\%$  on average for all the municipal cities in China [46] (Fig. 5). Reductions for the three key regions were even more significant:  $40\%$  for BTH,  $34\%$  for YRD, and  $28\%$  for PRD. The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration in the PRD region was  $34~\mu \mathrm{g}\cdot \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , which adhered to NAAQS for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . This progress has motivated some local governments to actively target more stringent limits on  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$

(b)  
Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  $8\mathrm{hO}_3$  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.

metadata.title: Fig. 2.  PM_{2.5}  distribution at city-level in 2014 to 2020. The frequency histograms in (a)-(g) denote the frequency distribution of  PM_{2.5}  in 368 cities across mainland China. The dot lines denote the air quality targets of WHO (i.e., IT-1, IT-2, IT-3, IT-4 and AQG of annual mean concentrations of 35, 25, 15, 10, and  5\mu gm^{-3} ).
references: In 2014, only  $26.63\%$  cities in mainland China met the WHO Interim target (IT)-1 standard, which is also the Chinese National Ambient Air Quality Standard level 2 (CNAQS II, annual mean  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  of  $35~\mu \mathrm{g}~\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) (Fig. 2). By contrast,  $44.83\%$  cities met the IT-1 standard at the end of APPCAP. However, few cities ( $\sim 0.05\%$ ) met the IT-3 standard (also the CNAQS level 1, annual mean  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  of  $15~\mu \mathrm{g}~\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) in APPCAP. In 2020, the air quality was further improved, with nearly  $3/4$  ( $\sim 71.82\%$ ) cities meeting the IT-1 standard and  $4.07\%$  cities meeting the IT-3 standard. However, few cities met the WHO IT-4 level (annual mean  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  of  $10~\mu \mathrm{g}~\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) and no city met the updated WHO AQG (annual mean  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  of  $5~\mu \mathrm{g}~\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) in 2020, suggesting that the threat of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution is still prevalent in China, despite the remarkable achievements have been made through the implementation of ACP.

metadata.source_md: How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu.md
metadata.save_path: C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu\figure_4.jpg
metadata.title: Fig. 4. Spatial distribution (at  1km  resolution) of the  PM_{2.5} -attributable premature death in mainland China (a), BTH (b), YRD (c), FWP (d), and PRD region (e) in 2020.
references: The number of premature deaths attributable to ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  decreased by  $\sim 0.07$  million  $(5.00\%)$ , from 1.35 million  $(95\%$  Confidence Interval (CI): 1.06-1.78) to 1.28 million  $(95\%$  CI: 0.97-1.73) in the APPCAP (2014 to 2017) (Fig. 6a and Fig. S9) and showed a continuous declining trend, decreasing to 1.12 million  $(95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) by 2020 (Fig. 4 and Fig. 6a). The number of attributable premature deaths in most areas  $(\sim 99.5\%)$  of China is less than four cases per  $1\mathrm{km}^2$  in 2020 (Fig. 4). Large numbers of premature deaths

were identified in dense populations and advanced industrial areas in the BTH, YRD, PRD and FWP regions (Fig. 4 and Table 1), despite the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in these regions decreased over time. The high-density premature death values are mostly found in metropolitan cities such as Beijing, Shanghai and Guangzhou. The largest number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths per unit area ( $1\mathrm{km}$ ) occurred in the FWP region. The  $4.72\%$  region of PRD had more than four cases of premature death per  $\mathrm{km}^2$ , which is higher than other three priority control regions ( $1.76\%$ ,  $2.47\%$ , and  $0.99\%$  in BTH, YRD, and FWP, respectively) and the national average ( $0.35\%$ ), indicating that the high population density also exacerbates  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths.

The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths was reduced in  $68.38\%$  areas of mainland China over 2014 to 2020 (Fig. 5). The most effectively controlled region was BTH, where over  $74\%$  of the area showed a decline in the number premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure. The FWP produced the smallest decrease amongst the four priority control regions, with the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths reducing in only  $26.95\%$  of the area. Regarding the decrease number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, unlike the largest decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration occurred in BTH region, the largest decrease in the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature death occurred in YRD region, with a decrease number of 0.037 million  $(17.00\%)$  in 2020 compared to 2014 (Table 1), followed by the PRD (decrease  $16.45\%$ ), BTH (decrease  $10.51\%$ ) and FWP (decrease  $8.99\%$ ) regions.

108°0'0"E  
Fig. 5. Spatial distribution of changes in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature death in mainland China (a), BTH (b), YRD (c), FWP (d), PRD region (e) between 2014 and 2020 (where the numbers of premature deaths in 2014 and 2020 are both zero this is represented by a white background).

We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\sim 27.05\%$  of global ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\%$  and  $26\%$  decrease in the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors.

metadata.save_path: C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\figure_2.jpg
metadata.title: Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.
references: The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of six gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 2. There was only one China III gasoline vehicle and its  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $1.675\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Three gasoline vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $1.452\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of other two China V gasoline vehicles was  $0.783\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of gasoline vehicles decreased gradually with the increasing emission standards. Four diesel vehicles were tested in this experiment as well. Only one China III diesel vehicle was tested and its  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $33.095\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Two diesel vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $15.134\mathrm{mg / km}$ . There was only one China V diesel vehicle and its  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $2.994\mathrm{mg / km}$ . As the emission standards rose up from China III to China V, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles decreased by more than  $90\%$ . As emission standards became stricter, the emission factor of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  gradually decreased because vehicles with higher emission standards usually had better exhaust control devices. Meanwhile, vehicles with higher emission standards were often younger than vehicles with lower emission standards (Zhang et al., 2019a, b). Under the same emission standards, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of diesel vehicles were obviously higher than gasoline vehicles, because the molecules that made up diesel were usually larger than gasoline (Distillation range of gasoline and diesel see Table s5.), and the engines could not be evenly mixed with air during

Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.

As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\%$  of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.

from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.

$\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were collected by the SEMTECH-PFS sub-module. SEMTECH-PFS sub-module had three channels for membrane sampling, where three sampling membranes with a diameter of  $47\mathrm{mm}$  were located. These three channels could not be used to collect  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  at the same time. When one channel was ventilated and sampling, the other two channels were closed. Computer was used to decide the channel of the airflow. Before sampling started, a quartz filter (MK360, Munktel, Sweden) was placed on Channel 1, a Teflon filter (R2PJ047, PALL, USA) was placed on Channel 2 and another quartz filter was placed on Chanel 3. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 1 was mainly used for analyzing organic carbon, elemental carbon and water-soluble ions. The Teflon filter membrane on Chanel 2 was used for analyzing elements. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 3 was used as the blank sample.

Carbonaceous aerosols consisted of organic carbon (OC), elemental carbon (EC), and a small amount of carbonate. Since carbonates were very small part in atmospheric particles and the proportion of carbonates was less than  $5\%$  of total carbon (TC), they would not be discussed in this study. The DRI thermal carbon analyzer (IMPROVE-A, DRI, USA) was used to detect the concentration of OC and EC, when  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  sample was collected without the pretreatment after equilibrium weighing. Several water-soluble ions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were analyzed, which included  $\mathrm{K}^{+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  and  $\mathrm{F}^-$ . Ion chromatograph (IC) was used to analyze the water-soluble ions that collected on the filter. 21 crustal elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). The mass concentration of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from vehicle emissions could be obtained through the CPM sub-module in SEMTECH-ECOSTAR. Combining the exhaust gas flow data recorded by the exhaust gas flow meter and the vehicle driving conditions data recorded by the GPS, the emission rate under the actual driving conditions could be calculated. According to the driving speed, the driving conditions of the tested vehicles were divided into eight speed segments: idle speed (0 km/h), 0–10 km/h, 0–20 km/h, 20–30 km/h, 30–40 km/h, 40–50 km/h, 50–60 km/h and >60 km/h. The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of the tested vehicles in all the eight speed segments were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3.
Table 2 Emission factors of carbonaceous components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles.

In Table 2, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V gasoline vehicles were 1086.15, 1054.06 and  $397.94~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $2.95\%$  and  $63.36\%$ . Meanwhile, it could be found that the OC emission factors of the tested gasoline vehicles were 8.68 times larger than those of EC except Vehicle 1#. TC emission factor of diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. To find out the TC emission characteristics of diesel vehicles with different emission standards, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V vehicles were 25,973.93, 11,745.64 and  $1735.88~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III diesel vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $54.78\%$  and  $93.32\%$ . OC emission factors of diesel vehicles were also 1.06 times larger than EC emission factors, which was similar to the OC and EC emission characteristics of gasoline vehicles.

The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the

The order of the water-soluble ion emission factors in the China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \mathrm{Na}^{+} > \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\mathrm{F}^{-} > \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$  as shown in Fig. 5a. The emission factors of  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$  in the top three positions were  $123.50~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $73.51~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $62.65~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV gasoline vehicles were  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and  $\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$ , which were  $56.71~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $26.84~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $20.12~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V gasoline vehicles were  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , and their emission factors were  $52.28~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.91~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $21.70~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. With the exception of  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , the emission factors of the tested water-soluble ions decreased gradually as the emission standard increased, which was consistent with the trend of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  changing with emission standards. As could be seen from Fig. 5b that the order of the water-soluble ions emission factors in China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \mathrm{Na}^{+} > \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+} > \mathrm{F}^{-}$ . The average emission factors of  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , and  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$  in the top three were  $1194.27~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $514.26~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $359.95~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV diesel vehicles were  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ , which were  $844.52~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $487.50~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $456.73~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V diesel vehicles were  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$ , and their emission factors were  $167.63~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $110.68~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $71.82~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.

From the perspective of changes in emission standards, the emission factors of  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  decreased as the emission standards increased. While the emission factors of  $\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\mathrm{F}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  increased to the top in China IV diesel vehicles. The percentages of  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles were  $2.258\%$ ,  $1.788\%$ ,  $0.773\%$ ,  $2.186\%$  and  $0.623\%$ , respectively. While their percentages from diesel vehicles were  $2.683\%$ ,  $0.789\%$ ,  $1.782\%$ ,  $2.918\%$  and  $0.857\%$ , respectively. The percentage of water-soluble ions in this study (13.12% in gasoline vehicles and 14.53% in diesel vehicles) were higher than  $10\%$  reported by Zhang et al. (2015), whose research was conducted in Beijing, a relatively dry city. However, the kinds of top water-soluble ions and the orders of them were similar to this study. The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.

It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ .

The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb} > \mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.

It could be found out from Fig. 6 that the emission factors of Na, Mg, K, and Ca were obviously larger than those of other inorganic elements for both gasoline and diesel vehicles. This mainly because the fuel was prepared with additives which contained Na, Mg, K, while Ca. Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and some other inorganic elements had lower emission factors, because these mainly came from a small amount of additives, catalysts and partly from the wear during engine operation. The percentages of inorganic elements were less than  $1.0\%$  both in gasoline and diesel vehicles, which was much smaller than  $27.73\%$  that reported by

The data of Table 3 showed that OC and EC were the two types of substances that accounted for the largest proportion of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in vehicle emissions. The proportion of OC in gasoline and diesel vehicles were  $53.926\%$  and  $51.151\%$ , respectively. The proportion of EC in diesel vehicles (25.169%) was nearly twice as much as gasoline vehicles (13.068%), which indicated more EC components were emitted from diesel vehicles than gasoline vehicles.  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  were the water-soluble ions with the top five proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. The proportion of  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  in gasoline vehicles was more than twice of diesel vehicles, while the proportion of  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  in diesel vehicles was more than twice of gasoline vehicles. These might be because the additives in gasoline and diesel vehicles were different. The additives in gasoline vehicles might contain more Ca, while additives in diesel vehicles might contain more Mg. K, Na, Ca, Mg, Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were the inorganic elements with higher proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. K, Na, Ca, and Mg were mainly derived from fuel additives, while Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were mainly derived from the wear and tear of vehicles engines.

Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. Emission factor of carbonaceous components emitted from diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. However, the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was over twice of diesel vehicles. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder.

(a) the proportion of six diseases in total deaths, and (b) the number of deaths in different ages (stroke: cerebrovascular disease; DM: type II diabetes; IHD: ischemic heart disease; LC: lung cancer; LRI: lower respiratory infections; COPD: chronic obstructive pulmonary disease).
references: The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths were primarily caused by stroke (cerebrovascular disease) and lung cancer (LC) in China, accounting for  $41.12\%$  and  $30.55\%$  of the total number of premature deaths, respectively (Fig. 6a). This was followed by chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and ischemic heart disease (IHD), which accounted for  $14.4\%$  and  $9.6\%$  of premature deaths, accordingly. The proportion of deaths from each disease was fairly stable between 2014 and 2020. The proportion of deaths due to ischemic heart disease (IHD), lung cancer (LC) and lower respiratory infections (LRI) increased slightly by  $1.41\%$ ,  $2.81\%$ , and  $0.58\%$ , while the proportion of deaths caused by stroke, DM and COPD decreased slightly by  $2.35\%$ ,  $0.27\%$  and  $2.19\%$ .

metadata.title: Fig. 6. Emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in  PM_{2.5} .
references: It could be found out from Fig. 6 that the emission factors of Na, Mg, K, and Ca were obviously larger than those of other inorganic elements for both gasoline and diesel vehicles. This mainly because the fuel was prepared with additives which contained Na, Mg, K, while Ca. Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and some other inorganic elements had lower emission factors, because these mainly came from a small amount of additives, catalysts and partly from the wear during engine operation. The percentages of inorganic elements were less than  $1.0\%$  both in gasoline and diesel vehicles, which was much smaller than  $27.73\%$  that reported by

Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.

from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.
# 3.1. Emission factors and rates of  $PM_{2,5}$
# 3.4. Emission characteristics of inorganic elements in  $PM_{2.5}$

The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.

It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ .

The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb} > \mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.

Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. Emission factor of carbonaceous components emitted from diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. However, the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was over twice of diesel vehicles. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder.

The water-soluble ions with the top four emission factors was  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ . Their percentages in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased slightly with high humidity and the order of top water-soluble ions kept the same. Their emission factors of diesel vehicles were all larger than gasoline vehicles. The highest emission factors of inorganic elements in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were K, Ca, Na and Mg, while Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and other inorganic elements had lower emission factors.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan was obtained and the largest proportion was OC. It occupied  $53.93\%$  and  $51.15\%$  in gasoline and diesel vehicles, respectively. Secondly, EC occupied  $13.07\%$  in gasoline vehicles while  $25.17\%$  in diesel vehicles.

metadata.title: Fig. 7. Major air pollution control goals, policies and specific control measures in China from the end of 2013 to 2020.
references: The control goals, policies, and specific measures in China during ACP are characterized in Fig. 7. Specifically, for the source control, a series of stringent industry emission standards were enacted to limit energy and industrial emissions (e.g., thermal power, iron and steel, and cement) for controlling energy consumption. Regarding the process control, clean production (e.g., using end-of-pipe devices in coal-fired power plants) was the major measure. In addition, implementation of ultra-low emission transformation of steel and other industries was advocated in APWBSD. The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions per coal and energy

China implemented a stringent Air Clean Plan (ACP) since 2013 to address environmental and health risks caused by ambient fine particulate matter  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . However, the policy effectiveness of ACP and co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures to environment and health are still largely unknown. Using satellite-based  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products produced in our previous study, concentration-response functions, and the logarithmic mean Divisia index (LMDI) method, we analyzed the spatiotemporal dynamics of premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and quantitatively estimated the policy benefits of ACP and carbon mitigation measures. We found the annual  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in China decreased by  $33.65\%$ $(13.41\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  from 2014 to 2020, accompanied by a decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths of 0.23 million  $(95\%$  confidence interval (CI): 0.22-0.27), indicating the huge benefits of China ACP for human health and environment. However, there were still 1.12 million  $(95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by the exposure of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in mainland China in 2020. Among all ACP measures, clean production (contributed  $55.98\%$  and  $51.14\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) and energy consumption control (contributed  $32.58\%$  and  $29.54\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) made the largest contribution during the past seven years. Nevertheless, the environmental and health benefits of ACP are not fully synergistic in different regions, and the effectiveness of ACP measures reduced from 2018 to 2020. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  has become one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction since 2018, confirming the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Our study suggests, with the saturation of clean production and source control, more targeted region-specific strategies and synergistic air pollution-carbon mitigation measures are critical to achieving the WHO's Air Quality Guideline target and the UN's Sustainable Development Goal Target in China.

Therefore, the Chinese government launched the strict Air Clean Plan (ACP) in 2013, aiming to address the above problem of severe air pollution. The ACP was divided into two phases: a five-year Air Pollution Prevention and Control Action Plan (APPCAP, 2013-2017) as well as a three-year Action Plan to Win Blue-Sky Defense (APWBSD, 2018-2020) (The State Council of China, 2013; 2018). The APPCAP was targeted to decrease  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations by over  $25\%$ ,  $20\%$ , and  $15\%$  by 2017 in the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei (BTH), Yangtze River Delta (YRD) and Pearl River Delta (PRD) regions, respectively (Fig. S1 and Table S1). The APWBSD, launched in 2018, set more comprehensive air quality requirements, such as the requirement that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations needed to be reduced by over  $18\%$  compared to 2015 in all cities at prefecture-level where the standard was not met. In this context, a series of effective air pollution control measures were proposed on energy consumption control, clean production and energy structure adjustment (Zhang et al., 2019a; Zheng et al., 2018). However, greater effort remains necessary and urgent to reduce the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations to the WHO level and the standard of "Beautiful China" vision (annual mean  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations  $\leq 35~\mu \mathrm{g}~\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  in all cities by 2035).

Although some pioneering research has explored improvements in air quality and health impacts due to the ACP (Yue et al., 2020; Geng et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019a; Liu et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019), these studies suffered from several limitations. First, current estimates of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths are limited in both spatial resolution and temporal scale. For example, Zhang et al. (2019a) estimated the change in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths in China from 2013 to 2017, but their analysis was limited to the national level. Yue et al. (2020) estimated pixel level premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure. However, the relative coarse spatial resolution (10 km) made it difficult to discern detailed information clearly such as hot spots of severe regions. Second, few previous studies focused on the environment and health benefits of specific measures for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control in the ACP in China (Cai et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2019). Zheng et al. (2018) estimated the effectiveness of ACP by measuring the related induced decrease of emitted pollutants (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  and  $\mathrm{NH_3}$ ). Geng et al. (2021) considered the effectiveness of end-of-pipe treatment in ACP, while ignoring the effect of other specific measures in ACP. Finally, carbon mitigation and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control have significant synergies (Li et al., 2019; Cheng et al., 2021; Xing et al., 2020; Tong et al., 2021). However, currently only a few studies considered or quantified the co-benefits of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  mitigation and reducing  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  so far. Some other studies projected

Fig. 3 shows the population exposed to ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in pollution control priority regions and in the whole nation in specific years at the beginning and end of the two phases of ACP (2014, 2017, and 2020). In 2014, over  $80\%$  of Chinese people lived in areas with  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  over  $35\mu \mathrm{g}$ $\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  (WHO IT-1). In 2020, the corresponding proportion decreased to  $40\%$ . BTH had the most severe  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, even with the most prominent air quality improvement amongst four priority regions. In 2014, almost all populations in BTH lived in areas where  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeded the WHO IT-1 threshold and this population proportion decreased to  $90\%$  in 2020. The FWP was not considered as a priority region for air pollution control in APPCAP, and pollution status was more serious during APPCAP as a result of crude industrial production and its gully terrain that was not conducive to the diffusion of pollutants. By contrast, the effect of ACP measures was profound in FWP region under APWBSD with a  $10\%$  reduction in the population exposed to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5} > 35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  in 2020 compared to 2017. The exposed population in PRD is relatively low compared to other priority regions, with  $24\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2014 and  $100\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2020, which also achieved the "Beautiful China" vision. However, almost all populations ( $\sim 98\%$  in the whole nation) still lived under the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeding the WHO IT-3 in 2020, not to mention the WHO AQG standard.

We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\sim 27.05\%$  of global ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\%$  and  $26\%$  decrease in the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors.

Our analysis of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  attributed deaths in China was compared with several recent studies (Table S3). Great progresses was made in previous studies (Zhang et al., 2019a; Li et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2019; McDuffie et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2017; Zou et al., 2019; Apte et al., 2015), albeit with several limitations in common: (1) previous studies focused mainly on the APPCAP (2013-2017) and lack the updated results of the APWBSD (2018-2020); (2) the chemical transport model was used widely in previous research, which showed advantages for predicting the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations without ground measurement, but also has limitations in terms of prediction accuracy (Ma et al., 2022); (3) the Integrated Exposure Response (IER) model used for premature death estimation in previous studies lacked sufficient information on deaths at high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and failed to separate exposure from ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and secondhand smoke, which could potentially introduce uncertainties (Burnett et al., 2014). Our research provides several advances compared to previous studies. First, we quantified the co-benefit of the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measures in China for the environment and health from 2014 to 2020. Second, we used state-of-the-art  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products with the highest accuracy (Wang et al., 2021; 2022) (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4 for detail) and the finest resolution (spatial resolution of  $1\mathrm{km}$ , Fig. S2) at the national scale to offer a full-coverage estimation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. With these innovations, we not only provided a more accurate estimation of the risk due to exposure to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , but also to analyze the great spatially heterogeneity of policy effectiveness. Third, we adopted the updated MR-BRT spline method, which fits the risk with a more flexible shape for more reliable estimation of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths (Hystad et al., 2020). The MR-BRT includes more exposure scenarios at high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels during fitting compared with other models such as the IER model (Yusuf et al., 2020; Chowdhury et al., 2022). It also excludes active smoking to control for and reduce potential bias due to this effect (Pope et al., 2018). Finally, our study estimated the co-benefit from  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  to the environment and health under the background of the ACP and NDC pledges for the first time.

Our study estimated the changes in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in China at a fine-resolution (1 km) during 2014-2020, identifying the measure with greatest environmental and health co-benefits in ACP, and demonstrating the potential of carbon mitigation measures for air quality improvement and public health enhancement. Results showed that (1) the average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in China decreased by  $33.65\%$  ( $13.41~\mu \mathrm{g}~\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) from 2014 to 2020, accompanied by a decrease in total attributed deaths of 0.23 million (95 % CI: 0.22-0.27), indicating the great benefits of China ACP for human health and environment. However, the premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China still reached 1.12 million (95 % CI: 0.79-1.56) in 2020. (2) Among China ACP measures, clean production and energy consumption control made the largest contribution to air quality improvements and health burden mitigation during the past seven years. However, the effectiveness of the two ACP measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. Meanwhile, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic spatially. (3) The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  has become one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase, confirming the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures.

metadata.concise_summary: The figure outlines China's major air pollution control goals, policies, and measures from 2013 to 2020, focusing on process reduction, structure reduction, and source reduction.
metadata.inferred_insight: The measures effectively decreased PM2.5 concentrations and associated premature deaths but highlighted the need for targeted strategies and mitigation measures for sustained benefits.

content.title: Fig. 3. Key milestones and policy evolution of air pollution control in China, including control actions on the emissions of  SO_2  and  NO_x  and on ambient concentrations of  PM_{2.5} . FYP: Five-Year Plan.
content.summary: The figure depicts the timeline and milestones in China's air pollution control policies, focusing on SO2, NOx emissions, and PM2.5 concentrations, highlighting significant control actions and achievements.
content.insight: The figure illustrates China's strategic evolution in pollution control, emphasizing comprehensive policies and multi-sector collaboration from emission control to broader air quality management, marking significant progress over the decades.
